toolUseResult.originalFile:
# hotloops - hot inner-loop detector

Detects the hottest loops in a RISC-V ELF by observing backward
translation-block transitions in QEMU. For each detected loop you get
the loop header PC, the backward-branch PC, and the iteration count.
With DWARF, you also get the source `file:line`.

Two parts ship together:

- `contrib/plugins/hotloops.c` - QEMU TCG plugin that records edges.
- `scripts/hotloops.sh` - wrapper that runs the plugin and formats output.

## Usage

```
scripts/hotloops.sh [options] <elf> [--] [program args...]
```

### Options

| Flag                | Default | Description                                                    |
|---------------------|---------|----------------------------------------------------------------|
| `--arch <a>`        | auto    | Target arch: `riscv32`, `riscv64`, or `auto` (from `file`).    |
| `--qemu <path>`     | auto    | Override the QEMU binary.                                      |
| `--plugin <path>`   | auto    | Override the plugin `.so`.                                     |
| `--limit <N>`       | 50      | Print at most N loops.                                         |
| `--min-iters <N>`   | 1       | Drop loops with fewer than N iterations (hot-loop threshold).  |
| `--objdump <path>`  | auto    | Override objdump (for `function+offset` resolution).           |
| `--addr2line <path>`| auto    | Override addr2line (for source line resolution).               |
| `--no-source`       | off     | Skip source resolution even if DWARF is present.               |
| `--raw`             | off     | Print raw plugin output (CSV-ish), no formatting.              |
| `--optimize-rvv`    | on      | Only report loops that contain at least one RVV instruction.   |
| `--no-optimize-rvv` | off     | Disable the RVV-only filter; report all loops.                 |
| `--llm`             | off     | Emit a Markdown report tuned for LLM consumption (see below).  |

### Build prerequisites

The plugin lives in `contrib/plugins/`. Build once:

```
ninja -C build contrib-plugins
```

It is incremental on subsequent edits to `hotloops.c`. The QEMU binaries
themselves (`qemu-riscv64`, `qemu-riscv32`) do **not** need to be rebuilt.

## How it detects a loop

For every translated block, the plugin remembers the PC of its last
instruction. When the next-executed block starts at a PC `<=` that
remembered PC, the transition is a backward edge - i.e. a loop iteration.
The pair `(header_pc, tail_pc)` is the key; the count is the number of
iterations.

This catches every loop a real backward branch closes (for/while/do-while,
goto, manually unrolled tails, etc.) without needing to model branch
semantics.

Caveat: each iteration counted is one *taken* backward branch. A loop
that runs N times has N-1 taken backward branches plus one fall-through
on exit, so the reported count is N-1 per call to the enclosing function.

## Output format

Header line, then a fixed-width table sorted by `iters` descending:

```
# hot loops (iters >= <threshold>), sorted desc, top <K>
iters | start_pc           -> end_pc             | location              | source
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
<n>   | 0x<16-hex>         -> 0x<16-hex>         | <fn>+0x<o> -> <fn>+0x<o> | file:line -> file:line
...
```

Columns:

- `iters`     - taken backward-branch count (sorted desc).
- `start_pc`  - PC of the loop header (the branch target).
- `end_pc`    - PC of the backward branch (the loop tail).
- `location`  - `function+offset -> function+offset` from the ELF symbol table.
                Shows `?` if the address is not inside any sized FUNC symbol.
- `source`    - `file:line -> file:line` from DWARF. Collapsed to a single
                `file:line` when both ends map to the same source line.
                Only present when the ELF has debug info and `--no-source`
                is not set.

The `--raw` mode prints the unformatted plugin output instead:

```
hotloops: <distinct> distinct backward edges, <hot> hot (>= <thr> iters), sorted by iterations desc
start_pc, end_pc, iterations
0x..., 0x..., <n>
...
```

## Example

Source:

```c
/* /tmp/hotloops_demo.c */
#include <stdio.h>

__attribute__((noinline))
static int dot(volatile const int *a, volatile const int *b, int n) {
    int s = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) s += a[i] * b[i];   /* line 6 */
    return s;
}

int main(void) {
    int a[64], b[64];
    for (int i = 0; i < 64; i++) { a[i] = i; b[i] = 64 - i; }
    long total = 0;
    for (int outer = 0; outer < 500; outer++)
        total += dot(a, b, 64);                      /* 64 * 500 inner iters */
    printf("total=%ld\n", total);
    return 0;
}
```

Build with debug info and run:

```
$ riscv64-linux-gnu-gcc -O1 -g -static /tmp/hotloops_demo.c -o /tmp/hotloops_demo
$ scripts/hotloops.sh --limit 5 --min-iters 100 /tmp/hotloops_demo
```

Output:

```
# hot loops (iters >= 100), sorted desc, top 5
iters | start_pc           -> end_pc             | location                                                                | source
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
31500 | 0x000000000001056e -> 0x000000000001057c | dot+0xc -> dot+0x1a                                                     | hotloops_demo.c:6 (discriminator 3) -> hotloops_demo.c:6 (discriminator 1)
 1757 | 0x000000000004ab54 -> 0x000000000004abe8 | read_encoded_value_with_base+0x70 -> read_encoded_value_with_base+0x104 | unwind-pe.h:265 -> unwind-pe.h:255
  876 | 0x000000000004bc5e -> 0x000000000004bd00 | classify_object_over_fdes+0x46 -> classify_object_over_fdes+0xe8        | unwind-dw2-fde.c:738 -> unwind-dw2-fde.c:732 (discriminator 1)
  876 | 0x000000000004aae4 -> 0x000000000004bcdc | read_encoded_value_with_base+0x0 -> classify_object_over_fdes+0xc4      | unwind-pe.h:203 -> unwind-dw2-fde.c:781
  869 | 0x000000000004bcf2 -> 0x000000000004bd64 | classify_object_over_fdes+0xda -> classify_object_over_fdes+0x14c       | unwind-dw2-fde.c:786 -> unwind-dw2-fde.c:793
```

Reading the top row:

- `iters = 31500` - close to the expected `64 * 500 = 32000`; the missing
  500 are the per-call exits (last iter of `dot` falls through instead of
  branching back).
- `start_pc = 0x1056e`, `end_pc = 0x1057c` - the header and backward
  branch of the inner loop.
- `location` - both PCs are inside `dot`, header at `+0xc`, tail at `+0x1a`.
- `source` - both map to `hotloops_demo.c:6`, which is exactly
  `for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) s += a[i] * b[i];`.

The remaining rows are glibc unwinder loops that ran during static init.
The 500-iter outer loop (`main+0x38 -> main+0x3a`) was filtered out by
`--min-iters 100` - well below the inner loop's contribution.

## Tips

- Strip rarely-interesting libc/startup loops by raising `--min-iters`
  to a value above your background noise floor (e.g. `--min-iters 1000`
  for a workload that runs your hot path millions of times).
- For a very short workload, lower `--min-iters` and raise `--limit`
  to see the full distribution.
- Pass program arguments after `--`, e.g.
  `scripts/hotloops.sh ./prog -- --bench fast`.
- `--raw` is useful when piping into another tool; the format is stable.

+- One `### L<N>` fact-sheet per reported loop (sorted by iters desc), with:

+- Indirect calls (`jalr` through a register) appear as `(indirect)`.

content.text:
Everything wired up. Summary of `--llm` mode:

**What it does** (verified by smoke test with a fake nested loop + RVV body):
- Parses plugin output, infers parent/child via PC-range containment.
- Emits a Markdown report:
  - Header: `target`, `total_iters`, `reported_loops`, `min_iters`, `rvv_filter`.
  - `## TL;DR`: one templated sentence naming the hottest loop, its function, source line, percentage, RVV-or-scalar, and nesting status.
  - `## Loops`: one `### L<N>` fact-sheet per loop with `iters`, `pc_range`, `function`, `source`, inline `code:` snippet, `rvv_insns:` listing (cap 8), `parent`/`children` (stable IDs), and `calls:` (direct `jal`/`jalr` callees from operands).

**Implementation pieces**:
- `--llm` flag, default off; orthogonal to `--optimize-rvv` and `--no-source`.
- Wider disasm capture (full operands) when `--llm` or `--optimize-rvv` is on.
- Dropped `addr2line -s` so we have full paths for source-file lookup; basename in Python preserves the table mode's display.
- TL;DR is generated by the script (template), not an LLM call - deterministic, offline, reproducible.

**Known limits** (documented):
- Nesting via PC-range catches only same-function nesting; cross-function "nesting" via call shows as separate loops linked through the `calls:` field.
- Indirect `jalr` shows `(indirect)`.
- `code:` snippets require the DWARF source path to be reachable on disk.

# hotloops - hot inner-loop detector

Detects the hottest loops in a RISC-V ELF by observing backward
translation-block transitions in QEMU. For each detected loop you get
the loop header PC, the backward-branch PC, and the iteration count.
With DWARF, you also get the source `file:line`.

Two parts ship together:

- `contrib/plugins/hotloops.c` - QEMU TCG plugin that records edges.
- `scripts/hotloops.sh` - wrapper that runs the plugin and formats output.

## Usage

```
scripts/hotloops.sh [options] <elf> [--] [program args...]
```

### Options

| Flag                | Default | Description                                                    |
|---------------------|---------|----------------------------------------------------------------|
| `--arch <a>`        | auto    | Target arch: `riscv32`, `riscv64`, or `auto` (from `file`).    |
| `--qemu <path>`     | auto    | Override the QEMU binary.                                      |
| `--plugin <path>`   | auto    | Override the plugin `.so`.                                     |
| `--limit <N>`       | 50      | Print at most N loops.                                         |
| `--min-iters <N>`   | 1       | Drop loops with fewer than N iterations (hot-loop threshold).  |
| `--objdump <path>`  | auto    | Override objdump (for `function+offset` resolution).           |
| `--addr2line <path>`| auto    | Override addr2line (for source line resolution).               |
| `--no-source`       | off     | Skip source resolution even if DWARF is present.               |
| `--raw`             | off     | Print raw plugin output (CSV-ish), no formatting.              |
| `--optimize-rvv`    | on      | Only report loops that contain at least one RVV instruction.   |
| `--no-optimize-rvv` | off     | Disable the RVV-only filter; report all loops.                 |
| `--llm`             | off     | Emit a Markdown report tuned for LLM consumption (see below).  |

### Build prerequisites

The plugin lives in `contrib/plugins/`. Build once:

```
ninja -C build contrib-plugins
```

It is incremental on subsequent edits to `hotloops.c`. The QEMU binaries
themselves (`qemu-riscv64`, `qemu-riscv32`) do **not** need to be rebuilt.

## How it detects a loop

For every translated block, the plugin remembers the PC of its last
instruction. When the next-executed block starts at a PC `<=` that
remembered PC, the transition is a backward edge - i.e. a loop iteration.
The pair `(header_pc, tail_pc)` is the key; the count is the number of
iterations.

This catches every loop a real backward branch closes (for/while/do-while,
goto, manually unrolled tails, etc.) without needing to model branch
semantics.

Caveat: each iteration counted is one *taken* backward branch. A loop
that runs N times has N-1 taken backward branches plus one fall-through

## Output format

Header line, then a fixed-width table sorted by `iters` descending:

```
# hot loops (iters >= <threshold>), sorted desc, top <K>
iters | start_pc           -> end_pc             | location              | source
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
<n>   | 0x<16-hex>         -> 0x<16-hex>         | <fn>+0x<o> -> <fn>+0x<o> | file:line -> file:line
...
```

Columns:

- `iters`     - taken backward-branch count (sorted desc).
- `start_pc`  - PC of the loop header (the branch target).
- `end_pc`    - PC of the backward branch (the loop tail).
- `location`  - `function+offset -> function+offset` from the ELF symbol table.

- `source`    - `file:line -> file:line` from DWARF. Collapsed to a single
                `file:line` when both ends map to the same source line.
                Only present when the ELF has debug info and `--no-source`

The `--raw` mode prints the unformatted plugin output instead:

```
hotloops: <distinct> distinct backward edges, <hot> hot (>= <thr> iters), sorted by iterations desc
start_pc, end_pc, iterations
0x..., 0x..., <n>
...
```

## LLM mode (`--llm`)

Use `--llm` to emit a Markdown report designed for an LLM to ingest. The
report is structured so the model can answer "what is the hottest loop?"
from the first paragraph, then drill down via stable IDs.

Layout:

- A header block: `target`, `total_iters`, `reported_loops`, `min_iters`,
  `rvv_filter`.
- A `## TL;DR` paragraph naming the hottest loop, its function and source
  line, percentage of total iterations, RVV vs scalar, and nesting.
- One `### L<N>` fact-sheet per reported loop (sorted by iters desc), with:
  `iters`, `pc_range`, `function`, `source`, an inline `code:` snippet
  pulled from the resolved source file (best-effort), `rvv:` summary plus

## Example

Source:

```c
/* /tmp/hotloops_demo.c */
#include <stdio.h>

__attribute__((noinline))
static int dot(volatile const int *a, volatile const int *b, int n) {
    int s = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) s += a[i] * b[i];   /* line 6 */
    return s;
}

int main(void) {
    int a[64], b[64];
    for (int i = 0; i < 64; i++) { a[i] = i; b[i] = 64 - i; }
    long total = 0;
    for (int outer = 0; outer < 500; outer++)
        total += dot(a, b, 64);                      /* 64 * 500 inner iters */
    printf("total=%ld\n", total);
    return 0;
}
```

Build with debug info and run:

```
$ riscv64-linux-gnu-gcc -O1 -g -static /tmp/hotloops_demo.c -o /tmp/hotloops_demo
$ scripts/hotloops.sh --limit 5 --min-iters 100 /tmp/hotloops_demo
```

Output:

```
# hot loops (iters >= 100), sorted desc, top 5
iters | start_pc           -> end_pc             | location                                                                | source
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
31500 | 0x000000000001056e -> 0x000000000001057c | dot+0xc -> dot+0x1a                                                     | hotloops_demo.c:6 (discriminator 3) -> hotloops_demo.c:6 (discriminator 1)
 1757 | 0x000000000004ab54 -> 0x000000000004abe8 | read_encoded_value_with_base+0x70 -> read_encoded_value_with_base+0x104 | unwind-pe.h:265 -> unwind-pe.h:255
  876 | 0x000000000004bc5e -> 0x000000000004bd00 | classify_object_over_fdes+0x46 -> classify_object_over_fdes+0xe8        | unwind-dw2-fde.c:738 -> unwind-dw2-fde.c:732 (discriminator 1)
  876 | 0x000000000004aae4 -> 0x000000000004bcdc | read_encoded_value_with_base+0x0 -> classify_object_over_fdes+0xc4      | unwind-pe.h:203 -> unwind-dw2-fde.c:781
  869 | 0x000000000004bcf2 -> 0x000000000004bd64 | classify_object_over_fdes+0xda -> classify_object_over_fdes+0x14c       | unwind-dw2-fde.c:786 -> unwind-dw2-fde.c:793
```

Reading the top row:

- `iters = 31500` - close to the expected `64 * 500 = 32000`; the missing
  500 are the per-call exits (last iter of `dot` falls through instead of
  branching back).
- `start_pc = 0x1056e`, `end_pc = 0x1057c` - the header and backward
  branch of the inner loop.
- `location` - both PCs are inside `dot`, header at `+0xc`, tail at `+0x1a`.
- `source` - both map to `hotloops_demo.c:6`, which is exactly
  `for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) s += a[i] * b[i];`.

The remaining rows are glibc unwinder loops that ran during static init.
The 500-iter outer loop (`main+0x38 -> main+0x3a`) was filtered out by
`--min-iters 100` - well below the inner loop's contribution.

## Tips

- Strip rarely-interesting libc/startup loops by raising `--min-iters`
  to a value above your background noise floor (e.g. `--min-iters 1000`
  for a workload that runs your hot path millions of times).
- For a very short workload, lower `--min-iters` and raise `--limit`
  to see the full distribution.
- Pass program arguments after `--`, e.g.
  `scripts/hotloops.sh ./prog -- --bench fast`.
- `--raw` is useful when piping into another tool; the format is stable.

lines: | `--raw`             | off     | Print raw plugin output (CSV-ish), no formatting.              |
| `--optimize-rvv`    | on      | Only report loops that contain at least one RVV instruction.   |
| `--no-optimize-rvv` | off     | Disable the RVV-only filter; report all loops.                 |
-| `--llm`             | off     | Emit a Markdown report tuned for LLM consumption (see below).  |
+| `--llm`             | on      | Emit a Markdown report tuned for LLM consumption (see below).  |
+| `--no-llm`          | off     | Emit the plain human-readable table instead of the LLM report. |

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Hot inner-loop detector wrapper.
#
# Runs an ELF under user-mode QEMU with the hotloops plugin and prints
# a sorted table of detected backward branches:
#
#   iters | start_pc -> end_pc | function:offset
#
# Usage:
#   scripts/hotloops.sh [opts] <elf> [--] [prog args...]
#
# Options:
#   --arch <riscv32|riscv64|auto>   Target arch (default: auto)
#   --qemu <path>                   Override qemu binary
#   --plugin <path>                 Override plugin .so
#   --limit <N>                     Top-N loops to print (default 50)
#   --min-iters <N>                 Hot-loop threshold; drop loops with
#                                   fewer than N iterations (default 1)
#   --objdump <path>                Override objdump (resolves symbols)
#   --addr2line <path>              Override addr2line (resolves source lines)
#   --no-source                     Disable source file:line resolution
#   --raw                           Print raw plugin output, no formatting
#   -h | --help                     Show help

set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd -- "$(dirname -- "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
QEMU_ROOT="$(cd -- "${SCRIPT_DIR}/.." && pwd)"

ARCH="auto"
QEMU=""
PLUGIN=""
LIMIT=50
MIN_ITERS=1
OBJDUMP=""
ADDR2LINE=""
NO_SOURCE=0
RAW=0

usage() { sed -n '2,20p' "$0"; exit 0; }

scripts/hotloops: add baremetal support, RVV filter, LLM report, and docs

Extend the hotloops wrapper script and document the tool.

Baremetal QEMU runner:
- Switch from user-mode to qemu-system-<arch> with -bios <elf>.
- Add --machine (default andes_ae350), --cpu (default
  andes-ax45mpv,vext_spec=v1.0,vlen=1024), --qemu-config (-readconfig),
  --cwd (chdir before launch so semihosting fread paths resolve), and
  --qemu-arg (repeatable passthrough).

RVV-only filter (--optimize-rvv, default on):
- Scan the ELF disassembly with objdump and drop loops whose
  [start_pc, end_pc] range contains no V-extension mnemonic (vsetvli,
  vle*, vse*, vadd*, ...). All detection lives in the script, so the
  TCG plugin remains unchanged. Use --no-optimize-rvv to disable.

LLM report mode (--llm, default on):
- Emit a Markdown report tuned for LLM consumption: header, TL;DR
  paragraph naming the hottest loop, and one per-loop fact-sheet block
  per reported edge with stable IDs (L1, L2, ...), pc_range, function,
  source file:line + inline code snippet, RVV instruction listing,
  parent/children loop IDs (inferred from PC-range containment), and
  direct callees (jal/jalr resolved via the <name> annotation). The
  TL;DR is templated from the data, no external call is made. Use
  --no-llm for the original table.

docs/hotloops.md:
- Describe the plugin, wrapper, both modes, output format, build
  prerequisites, and known limitations.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

content.content:
1	# hotloops - hot inner-loop detector
2	
3	Detects the hottest loops in a RISC-V ELF by observing backward
4	translation-block transitions in QEMU. For each detected loop you get
5	the loop header PC, the backward-branch PC, and the iteration count.
6	With DWARF, you also get the source `file:line`.
7	
8	Two parts ship together:
9	
10	- `contrib/plugins/hotloops.c` - QEMU TCG plugin that records edges.
11	- `scripts/hotloops.sh` - wrapper that runs the plugin and formats output.
12	
13	## Usage
14	
15	```
16	scripts/hotloops.sh [options] <elf> [--] [program args...]
17	```
18	
19	### Options
20	
21	| Flag                | Default | Description                                                    |
22	|---------------------|---------|----------------------------------------------------------------|
23	| `--arch <a>`        | auto    | Target arch: `riscv32`, `riscv64`, or `auto` (from `file`).    |
24	| `--qemu <path>`     | auto    | Override the QEMU binary.                                      |
25	| `--plugin <path>`   | auto    | Override the plugin `.so`.                                     |
26	| `--limit <N>`       | 50      | Print at most N loops.                                         |
27	| `--min-iters <N>`   | 1       | Drop loops with fewer than N iterations (hot-loop threshold).  |
28	| `--objdump <path>`  | auto    | Override objdump (for `function+offset` resolution).           |
29	| `--addr2line <path>`| auto    | Override addr2line (for source line resolution).               |
30	| `--no-source`       | off     | Skip source resolution even if DWARF is present.               |
31	| `--raw`             | off     | Print raw plugin output (CSV-ish), no formatting.              |
32	| `--optimize-rvv`    | on      | Only report loops that contain at least one RVV instruction.   |
33	| `--no-optimize-rvv` | off     | Disable the RVV-only filter; report all loops.                 |
34	| `--llm`             | on      | Emit a Markdown report tuned for LLM consumption (see below).  |
35	| `--no-llm`          | off     | Emit the plain human-readable table instead of the LLM report. |
36	
37	### Build prerequisites
38	
39	The plugin lives in `contrib/plugins/`. Build once:
40	
41	```
42	ninja -C build contrib-plugins
43	```
44	
45	It is incremental on subsequent edits to `hotloops.c`. The QEMU binaries
46	themselves (`qemu-riscv64`, `qemu-riscv32`) do **not** need to be rebuilt.
47	
48	## How it detects a loop
49	
50	For every translated block, the plugin remembers the PC of its last
51	instruction. When the next-executed block starts at a PC `<=` that
52	remembered PC, the transition is a backward edge - i.e. a loop iteration.
53	The pair `(header_pc, tail_pc)` is the key; the count is the number of
54	iterations.
55	
56	This catches every loop a real backward branch closes (for/while/do-while,
57	goto, manually unrolled tails, etc.) without needing to model branch
58	semantics.
59	
60	Caveat: each iteration counted is one *taken* backward branch. A loop
61	that runs N times has N-1 taken backward branches plus one fall-through
62	on exit, so the reported count is N-1 per call to the enclosing function.
63	
64	## Output format
65	
66	Header line, then a fixed-width table sorted by `iters` descending:
67	
68	```
69	# hot loops (iters >= <threshold>), sorted desc, top <K>
70	iters | start_pc           -> end_pc             | location              | source
71	-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
72	<n>   | 0x<16-hex>         -> 0x<16-hex>         | <fn>+0x<o> -> <fn>+0x<o> | file:line -> file:line
73	...
74	```
75	
76	Columns:
77	
78	- `iters`     - taken backward-branch count (sorted desc).
79	- `start_pc`  - PC of the loop header (the branch target).
80	- `end_pc`    - PC of the backward branch (the loop tail).
81	- `location`  - `function+offset -> function+offset` from the ELF symbol table.
82	                Shows `?` if the address is not inside any sized FUNC symbol.
83	- `source`    - `file:line -> file:line` from DWARF. Collapsed to a single
84	                `file:line` when both ends map to the same source line.
85	                Only present when the ELF has debug info and `--no-source`
86	                is not set.
87	
88	The `--raw` mode prints the unformatted plugin output instead:
89	
90	```
91	hotloops: <distinct> distinct backward edges, <hot> hot (>= <thr> iters), sorted by iterations desc
92	start_pc, end_pc, iterations
93	0x..., 0x..., <n>
94	...
95	```
96	
97	## LLM mode (`--llm`)
98	
99	Use `--llm` to emit a Markdown report designed for an LLM to ingest. The
100	report is structured so the model can answer "what is the hottest loop?"
101	from the first paragraph, then drill down via stable IDs.
102	
103	Layout:
104	
105	- A header block: `target`, `total_iters`, `reported_loops`, `min_iters`,
106	  `rvv_filter`.
107	- A `## TL;DR` paragraph naming the hottest loop, its function and source
108	  line, percentage of total iterations, RVV vs scalar, and nesting.
109	- One `### L<N>` fact-sheet per reported loop (sorted by iters desc), with:
110	  `iters`, `pc_range`, `function`, `source`, an inline `code:` snippet
111	  pulled from the resolved source file (best-effort), `rvv:` summary plus
112	  `rvv_insns:` listing (capped at 8), `parent:` / `children:` (PC-range
113	  containment - same-function nesting only), and `calls:` listing direct
114	  callees inside the loop body (`jal`/`jalr` with statically known target).
115	
116	Limitations:
117	
118	- Nesting via PC-range containment captures **only same-function nesting**.
119	  A caller's hot loop that calls a callee containing its own hot loop will
120	  show as two unrelated loops; follow `calls:` to bridge.
121	- Indirect calls (`jalr` through a register) appear as `(indirect)`.
122	- `code:` snippets require the source file to be reachable at the absolute
123	  path stored in DWARF; cross-compiled or relocated builds may fall back to
124	  just `file:line`.
125	
126	## Example
127	
128	Source:
129	
130	```c
131	/* /tmp/hotloops_demo.c */
132	#include <stdio.h>
133	
134	__attribute__((noinline))
135	static int dot(volatile const int *a, volatile const int *b, int n) {
136	    int s = 0;
137	    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) s += a[i] * b[i];   /* line 6 */
138	    return s;
139	}
140	
141	int main(void) {
142	    int a[64], b[64];
143	    for (int i = 0; i < 64; i++) { a[i] = i; b[i] = 64 - i; }
144	    long total = 0;
145	    for (int outer = 0; outer < 500; outer++)
146	        total += dot(a, b, 64);                      /* 64 * 500 inner iters */
147	    printf("total=%ld\n", total);
148	    return 0;
149	}
150	```
151	
152	Build with debug info and run:
153	
154	```
155	$ riscv64-linux-gnu-gcc -O1 -g -static /tmp/hotloops_demo.c -o /tmp/hotloops_demo
156	$ scripts/hotloops.sh --limit 5 --min-iters 100 /tmp/hotloops_demo
157	```
158	
159	Output:
160	
161	```
162	# hot loops (iters >= 100), sorted desc, top 5
163	iters | start_pc           -> end_pc             | location                                                                | source
164	-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
165	31500 | 0x000000000001056e -> 0x000000000001057c | dot+0xc -> dot+0x1a                                                     | hotloops_demo.c:6 (discriminator 3) -> hotloops_demo.c:6 (discriminator 1)
166	 1757 | 0x000000000004ab54 -> 0x000000000004abe8 | read_encoded_value_with_base+0x70 -> read_encoded_value_with_base+0x104 | unwind-pe.h:265 -> unwind-pe.h:255
167	  876 | 0x000000000004bc5e -> 0x000000000004bd00 | classify_object_over_fdes+0x46 -> classify_object_over_fdes+0xe8        | unwind-dw2-fde.c:738 -> unwind-dw2-fde.c:732 (discriminator 1)
168	  876 | 0x000000000004aae4 -> 0x000000000004bcdc | read_encoded_value_with_base+0x0 -> classify_object_over_fdes+0xc4      | unwind-pe.h:203 -> unwind-dw2-fde.c:781
169	  869 | 0x000000000004bcf2 -> 0x000000000004bd64 | classify_object_over_fdes+0xda -> classify_object_over_fdes+0x14c       | unwind-dw2-fde.c:786 -> unwind-dw2-fde.c:793
170	```
171	
172	Reading the top row:
173	
174	- `iters = 31500` - close to the expected `64 * 500 = 32000`; the missing
175	  500 are the per-call exits (last iter of `dot` falls through instead of
176	  branching back).
177	- `start_pc = 0x1056e`, `end_pc = 0x1057c` - the header and backward
178	  branch of the inner loop.
179	- `location` - both PCs are inside `dot`, header at `+0xc`, tail at `+0x1a`.
180	- `source` - both map to `hotloops_demo.c:6`, which is exactly
181	  `for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) s += a[i] * b[i];`.
182	
183	The remaining rows are glibc unwinder loops that ran during static init.
184	The 500-iter outer loop (`main+0x38 -> main+0x3a`) was filtered out by
185	`--min-iters 100` - well below the inner loop's contribution.
186	
187	## Tips
188	
189	- Strip rarely-interesting libc/startup loops by raising `--min-iters`
190	  to a value above your background noise floor (e.g. `--min-iters 1000`
191	  for a workload that runs your hot path millions of times).
192	- For a very short workload, lower `--min-iters` and raise `--limit`
193	  to see the full distribution.
194	- Pass program arguments after `--`, e.g.
195	  `scripts/hotloops.sh ./prog -- --bench fast`.
196	- `--raw` is useful when piping into another tool; the format is stable.
197

file.content:
# hotloops - hot inner-loop detector

Detects the hottest loops in a RISC-V ELF by observing backward
translation-block transitions in QEMU. For each detected loop you get
the loop header PC, the backward-branch PC, and the iteration count.
With DWARF, you also get the source `file:line`.

Two parts ship together:

- `contrib/plugins/hotloops.c` - QEMU TCG plugin that records edges.
- `scripts/hotloops.sh` - wrapper that runs the plugin and formats output.

## Usage

```
scripts/hotloops.sh [options] <elf> [--] [program args...]
```

### Options

| Flag                | Default | Description                                                    |
|---------------------|---------|----------------------------------------------------------------|
| `--arch <a>`        | auto    | Target arch: `riscv32`, `riscv64`, or `auto` (from `file`).    |
| `--qemu <path>`     | auto    | Override the QEMU binary.                                      |
| `--plugin <path>`   | auto    | Override the plugin `.so`.                                     |
| `--limit <N>`       | 50      | Print at most N loops.                                         |
| `--min-iters <N>`   | 1       | Drop loops with fewer than N iterations (hot-loop threshold).  |
| `--objdump <path>`  | auto    | Override objdump (for `function+offset` resolution).           |
| `--addr2line <path>`| auto    | Override addr2line (for source line resolution).               |
| `--no-source`       | off     | Skip source resolution even if DWARF is present.               |
| `--raw`             | off     | Print raw plugin output (CSV-ish), no formatting.              |
| `--optimize-rvv`    | on      | Only report loops that contain at least one RVV instruction.   |
| `--no-optimize-rvv` | off     | Disable the RVV-only filter; report all loops.                 |
| `--llm`             | on      | Emit a Markdown report tuned for LLM consumption (see below).  |
| `--no-llm`          | off     | Emit the plain human-readable table instead of the LLM report. |

### Build prerequisites

The plugin lives in `contrib/plugins/`. Build once:

```
ninja -C build contrib-plugins
```

It is incremental on subsequent edits to `hotloops.c`. The QEMU binaries
themselves (`qemu-riscv64`, `qemu-riscv32`) do **not** need to be rebuilt.

## How it detects a loop

For every translated block, the plugin remembers the PC of its last
instruction. When the next-executed block starts at a PC `<=` that
remembered PC, the transition is a backward edge - i.e. a loop iteration.
The pair `(header_pc, tail_pc)` is the key; the count is the number of
iterations.

This catches every loop a real backward branch closes (for/while/do-while,
goto, manually unrolled tails, etc.) without needing to model branch
semantics.

Caveat: each iteration counted is one *taken* backward branch. A loop
that runs N times has N-1 taken backward branches plus one fall-through
on exit, so the reported count is N-1 per call to the enclosing function.

## Output format

Header line, then a fixed-width table sorted by `iters` descending:

```
# hot loops (iters >= <threshold>), sorted desc, top <K>
iters | start_pc           -> end_pc             | location              | source
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
<n>   | 0x<16-hex>         -> 0x<16-hex>         | <fn>+0x<o> -> <fn>+0x<o> | file:line -> file:line
...
```

Columns:

- `iters`     - taken backward-branch count (sorted desc).
- `start_pc`  - PC of the loop header (the branch target).
- `end_pc`    - PC of the backward branch (the loop tail).
- `location`  - `function+offset -> function+offset` from the ELF symbol table.
                Shows `?` if the address is not inside any sized FUNC symbol.
- `source`    - `file:line -> file:line` from DWARF. Collapsed to a single
                `file:line` when both ends map to the same source line.
                Only present when the ELF has debug info and `--no-source`
                is not set.

The `--raw` mode prints the unformatted plugin output instead:

```
hotloops: <distinct> distinct backward edges, <hot> hot (>= <thr> iters), sorted by iterations desc
start_pc, end_pc, iterations
0x..., 0x..., <n>
...
```

## LLM mode (`--llm`)

Use `--llm` to emit a Markdown report designed for an LLM to ingest. The
report is structured so the model can answer "what is the hottest loop?"
from the first paragraph, then drill down via stable IDs.

Layout:

- A header block: `target`, `total_iters`, `reported_loops`, `min_iters`,
  `rvv_filter`.
- A `## TL;DR` paragraph naming the hottest loop, its function and source
  line, percentage of total iterations, RVV vs scalar, and nesting.
- One `### L<N>` fact-sheet per reported loop (sorted by iters desc), with:
  `iters`, `pc_range`, `function`, `source`, an inline `code:` snippet
  pulled from the resolved source file (best-effort), `rvv:` summary plus
  `rvv_insns:` listing (capped at 8), `parent:` / `children:` (PC-range
  containment - same-function nesting only), and `calls:` listing direct
  callees inside the loop body (`jal`/`jalr` with statically known target).

Limitations:

- Nesting via PC-range containment captures **only same-function nesting**.
  A caller's hot loop that calls a callee containing its own hot loop will
  show as two unrelated loops; follow `calls:` to bridge.
- Indirect calls (`jalr` through a register) appear as `(indirect)`.
- `code:` snippets require the source file to be reachable at the absolute
  path stored in DWARF; cross-compiled or relocated builds may fall back to
  just `file:line`.

## Example

Source:

```c
/* /tmp/hotloops_demo.c */
#include <stdio.h>

__attribute__((noinline))
static int dot(volatile const int *a, volatile const int *b, int n) {
    int s = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) s += a[i] * b[i];   /* line 6 */
    return s;
}

int main(void) {
    int a[64], b[64];
    for (int i = 0; i < 64; i++) { a[i] = i; b[i] = 64 - i; }
    long total = 0;
    for (int outer = 0; outer < 500; outer++)
        total += dot(a, b, 64);                      /* 64 * 500 inner iters */
    printf("total=%ld\n", total);
    return 0;
}
```

Build with debug info and run:

```
$ riscv64-linux-gnu-gcc -O1 -g -static /tmp/hotloops_demo.c -o /tmp/hotloops_demo
$ scripts/hotloops.sh --limit 5 --min-iters 100 /tmp/hotloops_demo
```

Output:

```
# hot loops (iters >= 100), sorted desc, top 5
iters | start_pc           -> end_pc             | location                                                                | source
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
31500 | 0x000000000001056e -> 0x000000000001057c | dot+0xc -> dot+0x1a                                                     | hotloops_demo.c:6 (discriminator 3) -> hotloops_demo.c:6 (discriminator 1)
 1757 | 0x000000000004ab54 -> 0x000000000004abe8 | read_encoded_value_with_base+0x70 -> read_encoded_value_with_base+0x104 | unwind-pe.h:265 -> unwind-pe.h:255
  876 | 0x000000000004bc5e -> 0x000000000004bd00 | classify_object_over_fdes+0x46 -> classify_object_over_fdes+0xe8        | unwind-dw2-fde.c:738 -> unwind-dw2-fde.c:732 (discriminator 1)
  876 | 0x000000000004aae4 -> 0x000000000004bcdc | read_encoded_value_with_base+0x0 -> classify_object_over_fdes+0xc4      | unwind-pe.h:203 -> unwind-dw2-fde.c:781
  869 | 0x000000000004bcf2 -> 0x000000000004bd64 | classify_object_over_fdes+0xda -> classify_object_over_fdes+0x14c       | unwind-dw2-fde.c:786 -> unwind-dw2-fde.c:793
```

Reading the top row:

- `iters = 31500` - close to the expected `64 * 500 = 32000`; the missing
  500 are the per-call exits (last iter of `dot` falls through instead of
  branching back).
- `start_pc = 0x1056e`, `end_pc = 0x1057c` - the header and backward
  branch of the inner loop.
- `location` - both PCs are inside `dot`, header at `+0xc`, tail at `+0x1a`.
- `source` - both map to `hotloops_demo.c:6`, which is exactly
  `for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) s += a[i] * b[i];`.

The remaining rows are glibc unwinder loops that ran during static init.
The 500-iter outer loop (`main+0x38 -> main+0x3a`) was filtered out by
`--min-iters 100` - well below the inner loop's contribution.

## Tips

- Strip rarely-interesting libc/startup loops by raising `--min-iters`
  to a value above your background noise floor (e.g. `--min-iters 1000`
  for a workload that runs your hot path millions of times).
- For a very short workload, lower `--min-iters` and raise `--limit`
  to see the full distribution.
- Pass program arguments after `--`, e.g.
  `scripts/hotloops.sh ./prog -- --bench fast`.
- `--raw` is useful when piping into another tool; the format is stable.

input.content:
# hotloops - hot inner-loop detector

Detects the hottest loops in a baremetal RISC-V ELF by observing backward
translation-block transitions in `qemu-system-<arch>`. For each detected
loop you get the loop header PC, the backward-branch PC, and the iteration
count. With DWARF, you also get the source `file:line`. With objdump
present, you get RVV-only filtering and per-loop disassembly context.

Two parts ship together:

- `contrib/plugins/hotloops.c` - QEMU TCG plugin that records edges.
- `scripts/hotloops.sh` - wrapper that runs the plugin and formats output.

## Usage

```
scripts/hotloops.sh [options] <elf>
```

The wrapper launches `qemu-system-<arch>` with `-bios <elf>` on a baremetal
machine (default `andes_ae350`) and a vector-capable CPU (default
`andes-ax45mpv,vext_spec=v1.0,vlen=1024`). Override per the options below
for other targets.

### Options

| Flag                | Default            | Description                                                    |
|---------------------|--------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------|
| `--arch <a>`        | auto               | Target arch: `riscv32`, `riscv64`, or `auto` (from `file`).    |
| `--qemu <path>`     | auto               | Override the `qemu-system-<arch>` binary.                      |
| `--plugin <path>`   | auto               | Override the plugin `.so`.                                     |
| `--machine <name>`  | `andes_ae350`      | `-M <name>` passed to QEMU.                                    |
| `--cpu <spec>`      | `andes-ax45mpv,...`| `-cpu <spec>` passed to QEMU. Default enables RVV 1.0 vlen=1024.|
| `--qemu-config <f>` | -                  | `-readconfig <file>` (e.g. AE350 device cfg).                  |
| `--cwd <dir>`       | -                  | `chdir` before launching QEMU (so semihosting `fread` of `bin/*.bin` resolves). |
| `--qemu-arg <arg>`  | -                  | Extra arg appended to the QEMU command. Repeatable.            |
| `--limit <N>`       | 50                 | Print at most N loops.                                         |
| `--min-iters <N>`   | 1                  | Drop loops with fewer than N iterations (hot-loop threshold).  |
| `--objdump <path>`  | auto               | Override objdump (for `function+offset` and disassembly).      |
| `--addr2line <path>`| auto               | Override addr2line (for source-line resolution).               |
| `--no-source`       | off                | Skip source resolution even if DWARF is present.               |
| `--raw`             | off                | Print raw plugin output (CSV-ish), no formatting.              |
| `--optimize-rvv`    | on                 | Only report loops that contain at least one RVV instruction.   |
| `--no-optimize-rvv` | off                | Disable the RVV-only filter; report all loops.                 |
| `--llm`             | on                 | Emit a Markdown report tuned for LLM consumption (see below).  |
| `--no-llm`          | off                | Emit the plain human-readable table instead of the LLM report. |

### Build prerequisites

The plugin lives in `contrib/plugins/`. Build once:

```
ninja -C build contrib-plugins
```

It is incremental on subsequent edits to `hotloops.c`. The QEMU binaries
themselves (`qemu-system-riscv64`, `qemu-system-riscv32`) do **not** need
to be rebuilt.

## How it detects a loop

For every translated block, the plugin remembers the PC of its last
instruction. When the next-executed block starts at a PC `<=` that
remembered PC, the transition is a backward edge - i.e. a loop iteration.
The pair `(header_pc, tail_pc)` is the key; the count is the number of
iterations.

This catches every loop a real backward branch closes (for/while/do-while,
goto, manually unrolled tails, etc.) without needing to model branch
semantics.

Caveat: each iteration counted is one *taken* backward branch. A loop
that runs N times has N-1 taken backward branches plus one fall-through
on exit, so the reported count is N-1 per call to the enclosing function.

## Output modes

Three output modes, in order of layered post-processing:

1. `--llm` (default on) - Markdown report tuned for LLM consumption.
2. `--no-llm` - plain human-readable table.
3. `--raw` - unformatted plugin output (overrides the above).

### LLM report (default)

The wrapper emits a single Markdown document structured so an LLM (or a
reader skimming top-down) can answer "what is the hottest loop?" from the
first paragraph, then drill down via stable IDs.

Layout:

- Header block: `target`, `total_iters`, `reported_loops`, `min_iters`,
  `rvv_filter`.
- `## TL;DR` paragraph naming the hottest loop, its function and source
  line, percentage of total iterations, RVV vs scalar (with the top-K
  unique vector mnemonics seen), and nesting status.
- One `### L<N>` fact-sheet per reported loop (sorted by iters desc):
  - `iters` and `pc_range` (`0xstart .. 0xend  (size: N B)`).
  - `function` (`fn+0xoff -> fn+0xoff`).
  - `source` (`file:line` or `file:line -> file:line` if the header and
    tail map to different lines).
  - `code` - inline best-effort source snippet read from the path stored
    in DWARF.
  - `rvv` summary plus `rvv_insns:` listing (capped at 8) with each
    `0xpc  mnemonic operands`.
  - `parent` / `children` - stable IDs of containing / contained loops.
    Inferred from PC-range containment, so this captures only
    same-function nesting.
  - `calls` - direct callees inside the loop body (`jal`/`jalr` with a
    statically known target via the `<name>` annotation). Indirect calls
    show as `(indirect)`.

The TL;DR is templated from the same data fields the per-loop blocks use;
no LLM call is made by the script itself.

Limitations:

- Same-function nesting only. A caller's hot loop that calls a callee
  containing its own hot loop will show as two unrelated loops; follow
  `calls:` to bridge.
- `code:` snippets require the source file to be reachable at the
  absolute path stored in DWARF. Cross-compiled or relocated builds may
  fall back to just `file:line`.
- The RVV filter is mnemonic-based: any insn whose mnemonic begins with
  `v<alpha>` (e.g. `vsetvli`, `vle32.v`, `vadd.vv`, `vmul.vv`). Standard
  scalar RISC-V has no such mnemonic, so this is exact in practice.

### Table mode (`--no-llm`)

Header line, then a fixed-width table sorted by `iters` descending:

```
# hot loops (iters >= <threshold>[, RVV-only]), sorted desc, top <K>
iters | start_pc           -> end_pc             | location              | source
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
<n>   | 0x<16-hex>         -> 0x<16-hex>         | <fn>+0x<o> -> <fn>+0x<o> | file:line -> file:line
...
```

Columns:

- `iters`     - taken backward-branch count (sorted desc).
- `start_pc`  - PC of the loop header (the branch target).
- `end_pc`    - PC of the backward branch (the loop tail).
- `location`  - `function+offset -> function+offset` from the ELF symbol
                table. Shows `?` if the address is not inside any sized
                FUNC symbol.
- `source`    - `file:line -> file:line` from DWARF (basenamed).
                Collapsed to a single `file:line` when both ends map to
                the same source line. Only present when the ELF has debug
                info and `--no-source` is not set.

### Raw mode (`--raw`)

Bypasses both the LLM report and the table, printing the unformatted
plugin output:

```
hotloops: <distinct> distinct backward edges, <hot> hot (>= <thr> iters), sorted by iterations desc
start_pc, end_pc, iterations
0x..., 0x..., <n>
...
```

Stable across releases - useful for piping into another tool.

## Example

A doubly-nested loop with an RVV inner body:

```c
/* hotloops_demo.c - baremetal entry */
#include <stdint.h>

__attribute__((noinline))
static int dot_rvv(const int *a, const int *b, int n) {
    int s = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) s += a[i] * b[i];   /* line 6, vectorized */
    return s;
}

void main(void) {
    static int a[64], b[64];
    for (int i = 0; i < 64; i++) { a[i] = i; b[i] = 64 - i; }
    long total = 0;
    for (int outer = 0; outer < 500; outer++)
        total += dot_rvv(a, b, 64);                  /* 64 * 500 inner iters */
    (void)total;
}
```

Build for AE350 with the Andes RISC-V toolchain (or any baremetal
RVV-capable toolchain), then run:

```
$ scripts/hotloops.sh --min-iters 100 --limit 5 build/hotloops_demo.elf
```

LLM-mode output (the default) looks like:

```markdown
# Hotloops Report
target:           hotloops_demo.elf
total_iters:      32000
reported_loops:   1
min_iters:        100
rvv_filter:       on

## TL;DR
Hottest loop is L1 in `dot_rvv` (hotloops_demo.c:6), 31500 iters = 98.4% of
total. Body is RVV (vsetvli, vle32.v, vmul.vv, +1 more). It is top-level
(no parent loop), and contains no inner loop.

## Loops

### L1 [hottest]  (98.4% of total)
iters:        31500
pc_range:     0x80000200 .. 0x80000218  (size: 24 B)
function:     dot_rvv+0x10 -> dot_rvv+0x28
source:       hotloops_demo.c:6
code:         for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) s += a[i] * b[i];   /* line 6, vectorized */
rvv:          yes (4 insns)
rvv_insns:
  - 0x80000200  vsetvli    t0,a2,e32,m1,ta,ma
  - 0x80000208  vle32.v    v1,(a0)
  - 0x8000020c  vmul.vv    v2,v1,v3
  - 0x80000210  vadd.vv    v4,v4,v2
parent:       -
children:     []
calls:        []
```

Reading the report:

- The TL;DR alone identifies the hot path: `dot_rvv` line 6, 98.4% of all
  taken backward branches, RVV-vectorized.
- `iters = 31500` is close to the expected `64 * 500 = 32000`; the missing
  500 are the per-call exits (last iter of `dot_rvv` falls through instead
  of branching back).
- The 500-iter outer loop in `main` was filtered out by `--min-iters 100`.
  It would also have been filtered by the default RVV-only filter since
  `main`'s body is scalar (drop with `--no-optimize-rvv` to see scalar
  loops too).

For piping into a different tool, use `--raw`:

```
$ scripts/hotloops.sh --raw build/hotloops_demo.elf | head
hotloops: 1 distinct backward edges, 1 hot (>= 1 iters), sorted by iterations desc
start_pc, end_pc, iterations
0x0000000080000200, 0x0000000080000218, 31500
```

## Tips

- Strip rarely-interesting startup loops by raising `--min-iters` to a
  value above your background noise floor (e.g. `--min-iters 1000` for a
  workload that runs your hot path millions of times).
- For a very short workload, lower `--min-iters` and raise `--limit`
  to see the full distribution.
- Default settings filter to RVV-containing loops only; pair with
  `--no-optimize-rvv` if you also want to see the scalar hot spots
  (e.g. an outer driver loop wrapping a vectorized kernel).
- For LLM consumption, pipe the default output straight into your prompt
  - the TL;DR is in the first ~10 lines and the per-loop blocks are
  cross-referenceable by their `L<N>` IDs.
- `--raw` is the stable interface for downstream tooling.

- `iters`     - taken backward-branch count (sorted desc).
- `start_pc`  - PC of the loop header (the branch target).
- `end_pc`    - PC of the backward branch (the loop tail).
- `location`  - `function+offset -> function+offset` from the ELF symbol
                table. Shows `?` if the address is not inside any sized
                FUNC symbol.
- `source`    - `file:line -> file:line` from DWARF (basenamed).
                Collapsed to a single `file:line` when both ends map to
                the same source line. Only present when the ELF has debug
                info and `--no-source` is not set.

### Raw mode (`--raw`)

Bypasses both the LLM report and the table, printing the unformatted
plugin output:

```
hotloops: <distinct> distinct backward edges, <hot> hot (>= <thr> iters), sorted by iterations desc
start_pc, end_pc, iterations
0x..., 0x..., <n>
...
```

Stable across releases - useful for piping into another tool.

## Example

A doubly-nested loop with an RVV inner body:

```c
/* hotloops_demo.c - baremetal entry */
#include <stdint.h>

__attribute__((noinline))
static int dot_rvv(const int *a, const int *b, int n) {
    int s = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) s += a[i] * b[i];   /* line 6, vectorized */
    return s;
}

void main(void) {
    static int a[64], b[64];
    for (int i = 0; i < 64; i++) { a[i] = i; b[i] = 64 - i; }
    long total = 0;
    for (int outer = 0; outer < 500; outer++)
        total += dot_rvv(a, b, 64);                  /* 64 * 500 inner iters */
    (void)total;
}
```

Build for AE350 with the Andes RISC-V toolchain (or any baremetal
RVV-capable toolchain), then run:

```
$ scripts/hotloops.sh --min-iters 100 --limit 5 build/hotloops_demo.elf
```

LLM-mode output (the default) looks like:

```markdown
# Hotloops Report
target:           hotloops_demo.elf
total_iters:      32000
reported_loops:   1
min_iters:        100
rvv_filter:       on

## TL;DR
Hottest loop is L1 in `dot_rvv` (hotloops_demo.c:6), 31500 iters = 98.4% of
total. Body is RVV (vsetvli, vle32.v, vmul.vv, +1 more). It is top-level
(no parent loop), and contains no inner loop.

## Loops

### L1 [hottest]  (98.4% of total)
iters:        31500
pc_range:     0x80000200 .. 0x80000218  (size: 24 B)
function:     dot_rvv+0x10 -> dot_rvv+0x28
source:       hotloops_demo.c:6
code:         for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) s += a[i] * b[i];   /* line 6, vectorized */
rvv:          yes (4 insns)
rvv_insns:
  - 0x80000200  vsetvli    t0,a2,e32,m1,ta,ma
  - 0x80000208  vle32.v    v1,(a0)
  - 0x8000020c  vmul.vv    v2,v1,v3
  - 0x80000210  vadd.vv    v4,v4,v2
parent:       -
children:     []
calls:        []
```

Reading the report:

- The TL;DR alone identifies the hot path: `dot_rvv` line 6, 98.4% of all
  taken backward branches, RVV-vectorized.
- `iters = 31500` is close to the expected `64 * 500 = 32000`; the missing
  500 are the per-call exits (last iter of `dot_rvv` falls through instead
  of branching back).
- The 500-iter outer loop in `main` was filtered out by `--min-iters 100`.
  It would also have been filtered by the default RVV-only filter since
  `main`'s body is scalar (drop with `--no-optimize-rvv` to see scalar
  loops too).

For piping into a different tool, use `--raw`:

```
$ scripts/hotloops.sh --raw build/hotloops_demo.elf | head
hotloops: 1 distinct backward edges, 1 hot (>= 1 iters), sorted by iterations desc
start_pc, end_pc, iterations
0x0000000080000200, 0x0000000080000218, 31500
```

## Tips

- Strip rarely-interesting startup loops by raising `--min-iters` to a
  value above your background noise floor (e.g. `--min-iters 1000` for a
  workload that runs your hot path millions of times).
- For a very short workload, lower `--min-iters` and raise `--limit`
  to see the full distribution.
- Default settings filter to RVV-containing loops only; pair with
  `--no-optimize-rvv` if you also want to see the scalar hot spots
  (e.g. an outer driver loop wrapping a vectorized kernel).
- For LLM consumption, pipe the default output straight into your prompt
  - the TL;DR is in the first ~10 lines and the per-loop blocks are
  cross-referenceable by their `L<N>` IDs.
- `--raw` is the stable interface for downstream tooling.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# hotloops - hot inner-loop detector

Detects the hottest loops in a RISC-V ELF by observing backward
translation-block transitions in QEMU. For each detected loop you get
the loop header PC, the backward-branch PC, and the iteration count.
With DWARF, you also get the source `file:line`.

Two parts ship together:

- `contrib/plugins/hotloops.c` - QEMU TCG plugin that records edges.
- `scripts/hotloops.sh` - wrapper that runs the plugin and formats output.

## Usage

```
scripts/hotloops.sh [options] <elf> [--] [program args...]
```

### Options

| Flag                | Default | Description                                                    |
|---------------------|---------|----------------------------------------------------------------|
| `--arch <a>`        | auto    | Target arch: `riscv32`, `riscv64`, or `auto` (from `file`).    |
| `--qemu <path>`     | auto    | Override the QEMU binary.                                      |
| `--plugin <path>`   | auto    | Override the plugin `.so`.                                     |
| `--limit <N>`       | 50      | Print at most N loops.                                         |
| `--min-iters <N>`   | 1       | Drop loops with fewer than N iterations (hot-loop threshold).  |
| `--objdump <path>`  | auto    | Override objdump (for `function+offset` resolution).           |
| `--addr2line <path>`| auto    | Override addr2line (for source line resolution).               |
| `--no-source`       | off     | Skip source resolution even if DWARF is present.               |
| `--raw`             | off     | Print raw plugin output (CSV-ish), no formatting.              |
| `--optimize-rvv`    | on      | Only report loops that contain at least one RVV instruction.   |
| `--no-optimize-rvv` | off     | Disable the RVV-only filter; report all loops.                 |
| `--llm`             | on      | Emit a Markdown report tuned for LLM consumption (see below).  |
| `--no-llm`          | off     | Emit the plain human-readable table instead of the LLM report. |

### Build prerequisites

The plugin lives in `contrib/plugins/`. Build once:

```
ninja -C build contrib-plugins
```

It is incremental on subsequent edits to `hotloops.c`. The QEMU binaries
themselves (`qemu-riscv64`, `qemu-riscv32`) do **not** need to be rebuilt.

## How it detects a loop

For every translated block, the plugin remembers the PC of its last
instruction. When the next-executed block starts at a PC `<=` that
remembered PC, the transition is a backward edge - i.e. a loop iteration.
The pair `(header_pc, tail_pc)` is the key; the count is the number of
iterations.

This catches every loop a real backward branch closes (for/while/do-while,
goto, manually unrolled tails, etc.) without needing to model branch
semantics.

Caveat: each iteration counted is one *taken* backward branch. A loop
that runs N times has N-1 taken backward branches plus one fall-through
on exit, so the reported count is N-1 per call to the enclosing function.

## Output format

Header line, then a fixed-width table sorted by `iters` descending:

```
# hot loops (iters >= <threshold>), sorted desc, top <K>
iters | start_pc           -> end_pc             | location              | source
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
<n>   | 0x<16-hex>         -> 0x<16-hex>         | <fn>+0x<o> -> <fn>+0x<o> | file:line -> file:line
...
```

Columns:

- `iters`     - taken backward-branch count (sorted desc).
- `start_pc`  - PC of the loop header (the branch target).
- `end_pc`    - PC of the backward branch (the loop tail).
- `location`  - `function+offset -> function+offset` from the ELF symbol table.
                Shows `?` if the address is not inside any sized FUNC symbol.
- `source`    - `file:line -> file:line` from DWARF. Collapsed to a single
                `file:line` when both ends map to the same source line.
                Only present when the ELF has debug info and `--no-source`
                is not set.

The `--raw` mode prints the unformatted plugin output instead:

```
hotloops: <distinct> distinct backward edges, <hot> hot (>= <thr> iters), sorted by iterations desc
start_pc, end_pc, iterations
0x..., 0x..., <n>
...
```

## LLM mode (`--llm`)

Use `--llm` to emit a Markdown report designed for an LLM to ingest. The
report is structured so the model can answer "what is the hottest loop?"
from the first paragraph, then drill down via stable IDs.

Layout:

- A header block: `target`, `total_iters`, `reported_loops`, `min_iters`,
  `rvv_filter`.
- A `## TL;DR` paragraph naming the hottest loop, its function and source
  line, percentage of total iterations, RVV vs scalar, and nesting.
- One `### L<N>` fact-sheet per reported loop (sorted by iters desc), with:
  `iters`, `pc_range`, `function`, `source`, an inline `code:` snippet
  pulled from the resolved source file (best-effort), `rvv:` summary plus
  `rvv_insns:` listing (capped at 8), `parent:` / `children:` (PC-range
  containment - same-function nesting only), and `calls:` listing direct
  callees inside the loop body (`jal`/`jalr` with statically known target).

Limitations:

- Nesting via PC-range containment captures **only same-function nesting**.
  A caller's hot loop that calls a callee containing its own hot loop will
  show as two unrelated loops; follow `calls:` to bridge.
- Indirect calls (`jalr` through a register) appear as `(indirect)`.
- `code:` snippets require the source file to be reachable at the absolute
  path stored in DWARF; cross-compiled or relocated builds may fall back to
  just `file:line`.

## Example

Source:

```c
/* /tmp/hotloops_demo.c */
#include <stdio.h>

__attribute__((noinline))
static int dot(volatile const int *a, volatile const int *b, int n) {
    int s = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) s += a[i] * b[i];   /* line 6 */
    return s;
}

int main(void) {
    int a[64], b[64];
    for (int i = 0; i < 64; i++) { a[i] = i; b[i] = 64 - i; }
    long total = 0;
    for (int outer = 0; outer < 500; outer++)
        total += dot(a, b, 64);                      /* 64 * 500 inner iters */
    printf("total=%ld\n", total);
    return 0;
}
```

Build with debug info and run:

```
$ riscv64-linux-gnu-gcc -O1 -g -static /tmp/hotloops_demo.c -o /tmp/hotloops_demo
$ scripts/hotloops.sh --limit 5 --min-iters 100 /tmp/hotloops_demo
```

Output:

```
# hot loops (iters >= 100), sorted desc, top 5
iters | start_pc           -> end_pc             | location                                                                | source
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
31500 | 0x000000000001056e -> 0x000000000001057c | dot+0xc -> dot+0x1a                                                     | hotloops_demo.c:6 (discriminator 3) -> hotloops_demo.c:6 (discriminator 1)
 1757 | 0x000000000004ab54 -> 0x000000000004abe8 | read_encoded_value_with_base+0x70 -> read_encoded_value_with_base+0x104 | unwind-pe.h:265 -> unwind-pe.h:255
  876 | 0x000000000004bc5e -> 0x000000000004bd00 | classify_object_over_fdes+0x46 -> classify_object_over_fdes+0xe8        | unwind-dw2-fde.c:738 -> unwind-dw2-fde.c:732 (discriminator 1)
  876 | 0x000000000004aae4 -> 0x000000000004bcdc | read_encoded_value_with_base+0x0 -> classify_object_over_fdes+0xc4      | unwind-pe.h:203 -> unwind-dw2-fde.c:781
  869 | 0x000000000004bcf2 -> 0x000000000004bd64 | classify_object_over_fdes+0xda -> classify_object_over_fdes+0x14c       | unwind-dw2-fde.c:786 -> unwind-dw2-fde.c:793
```

Reading the top row:

- `iters = 31500` - close to the expected `64 * 500 = 32000`; the missing
  500 are the per-call exits (last iter of `dot` falls through instead of
  branching back).
- `start_pc = 0x1056e`, `end_pc = 0x1057c` - the header and backward
  branch of the inner loop.
- `location` - both PCs are inside `dot`, header at `+0xc`, tail at `+0x1a`.
- `source` - both map to `hotloops_demo.c:6`, which is exactly
  `for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) s += a[i] * b[i];`.

The remaining rows are glibc unwinder loops that ran during static init.
The 500-iter outer loop (`main+0x38 -> main+0x3a`) was filtered out by
`--min-iters 100` - well below the inner loop's contribution.

## Tips

- Strip rarely-interesting libc/startup loops by raising `--min-iters`
  to a value above your background noise floor (e.g. `--min-iters 1000`
  for a workload that runs your hot path millions of times).
- For a very short workload, lower `--min-iters` and raise `--limit`
  to see the full distribution.
- Pass program arguments after `--`, e.g.
  `scripts/hotloops.sh ./prog -- --bench fast`.
- `--raw` is useful when piping into another tool; the format is stable.

content.text:
Doc updated to match the current tool state. Key changes:

- **Lead/Usage**: now reflects `qemu-system-<arch>` baremetal mode, dropped user-mode program-args.
- **Options table**: added the missing baremetal flags (`--machine`, `--cpu`, `--qemu-config`, `--cwd`, `--qemu-arg`).
- **Build prerequisites**: corrected QEMU binary names (`qemu-system-riscv64/32`).
- **Output modes section** restructured: LLM (default) -> table (`--no-llm`) -> raw, in order of post-processing layers.
- **Example**: rewritten as a baremetal hotloops_demo with an RVV inner loop, showing realistic LLM-mode Markdown output with stable IDs, RVV listings, nesting, and calls fields.
- **Tips**: dropped the obsolete program-args bullet, added LLM/RVV-filter interaction guidance.

Want me to commit and push?
